segments.s243-g193: 'ایا باد بگذر بایران زمین' is an address and command: 'ایا' here functions as an attention/vocative particle (classical 'O/Behold'), 'باد' is the noun 'wind' vocatively addressed, and 'بگذر' is an imperative verb meaning 'pass by / move on'. The phrase 'بایران زمین' is a compressed prepositional phrase meaning 'over/to the land of Iran' (classical ordering places the prepositional element adjacent to 'زمین' = 'land').
segments.s243-g194: 'پیامی بر از من بشاه گزین' is the second half of the command addressed to the wind. 'پیامی' is an indefinite object 'a message', 'بر' here functions as 'toward/to' marking the target of the message, 'از من' specifies the source 'from me', 'بشاه' means 'to the shah/king' (indirect object), and 'گزین' is an imperative 'choose/select' placed at the end — a final verb giving the instruction to pick or carry such a message.
segments.s243-g195: 'بگویش که بیژن بسختی درست' begins with the imperative 'بگویش' = بگو + ـش (enclitic = 'to him'), i.e. 'tell him'. 'که' introduces a reported clause 'that'. 'بیژن' is the clause subject. 'بسختی' is an adverbial phrase 'with great hardship / severely'. 'درست' here functions as the predicate describing Bijan's condition ('is fixed/held/in a dire state' in classical diction), so together 'بسختی درست' conveys 'is (held) in great distress'.
segments.s243-g196: 'چو آهو که در چنگ شیر نرست' is a simile introduced by 'چو' = 'like/as'. 'آهو' is the image 'gazelle'. The relative 'که' links to the subordinate clause 'در چنگ شیر نرست' — 'in the lion's grasp'. 'در' is the locative 'in', 'چنگ' is 'claw/grip', 'شیر' is 'lion', and 'نرست' here carries the meaning 'was not delivered/escaped' (classical sense 'to be delivered/saved'), so the whole clause means 'like a gazelle that was not freed from the lion's claws.'
segments.s243-g145: This noun phrase lists two kinds of fighters: گرازان (plural) + descriptive adjective/compound بدندان, followed by و and شیران بچنگ. بدندان is a bound adjective-like form meaning 'sharp‑toothed' or 'biting' modifying گرازان. بچنگ is a prepositional/relational form here ('with/clutching by the claw') modifying شیران; the order (noun + modifier) is typical for Persian poetic compounds.
segments.s243-g146: توانند is the present/subjunctive/modal sense 'are able / can'. کردن is the light verb here (infinitive) forming the verbal phrase with توانند — literally 'can do/perform.' بهر جای جنگ is a prepositional/locative phrase 'toward/for the place of battle' (بهر = toward/for, جای = place, جنگ = battle), so the clause means 'can act/engage at any place of battle.'
segments.s243-g147: یلان (plural) is a poetic plural for 'heroes' or 'braves'; هم here means 'also' or 'too.' بشمشیر is a prepositional/ablative compound meaning 'with the sword' (بـ + شمشیر). The sequence 'بشمشیر و تیر و کمان' lists weapons in the usual coordinated order 'sword, arrow and bow.'
segments.s243-g148: Again توانند is the modal 'are able.' کوشید is the infinitive of کوشیدن 'to strive/endeavor/attack' used with توانند as 'can strive/attack.' با بدگمان is an adverbial phrase 'with bad suspicion / ill‑will / ill‑disposition' — با = 'with' and بدگمان qualifying the manner. Word order places the modal + infinitive before the manner phrase.
segments.s243-g149: This noun phrase describes a single person: یکی 'one' (a certain one) followed by two adjectival phrases دست‌بسته 'hands‑bound' and برهنه تنا 'with a naked torso' (برهنه modifies تنا). Note the adjective‑first style common in poetry where multiple modifiers follow the numeral/indefinite.
segments.s243-g150: Another parallel nominal phrase: یکی را uses را as the definite/object marker (here marking the second 'one' as the item being described), ز = 'of/from' introducing the material, and پولاد پیراهنا = 'shirts of steel' (پولاد 'steel', پیراهنا 'shirts' or 'shirts/garments'), i.e. a warrior clad in steel‑mail. The contrast between the two 'one's is set up by the repeated یکی ... یکی.
segments.s243-g151: چگونه is an interrogative 'how/what good', introducing the rhetorical question. درد here is used idiomatically in the sense of 'use/benefit/worth' (rendered as 'what good'), followed by شیر 'lion' and the adjectival phrase بی چنگ تیز 'without a sharp claw' (بی = without, چنگ = claw, تیز = sharp). The noun phrase as a whole asks about the value/effectiveness of a lion lacking claws.
segments.s243-g152: اگر چند باشد is a concessive circumstantial clause 'even if / though it may be,' where اگر = 'if/even if', چند functions adverbially here ('however much / though'), and باشد is the subjunctive/present 'may be / is' used to set up the concession. دلش پر ستیز is a possessive phrase: دلش 'his heart' (دل + ـش) and پر ستیز 'full of strife/fighting' (پر = full (of), ستیز = strife/fight), qualifying the concession: 'even if his heart is full of fighting.'
segments.s243-g57: بپیچید is an imperative (2nd-person plural or polite) from پیچیدن meaning 'wrap/turn/twist'. بر خویشتن is a prepositional phrase with بر used poetically to indicate 'around/on oneself' (بر + خویشتن = 'around yourself'). بیژنا is a vocative proper name placed after the command (poetic positioning without an explicit vocative particle).
segments.s243-g58: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/because'), and چون here functions temporally as 'when/as'. رزم is the object 'battle/war' placed before the verb (typical Persian word order can front the object for emphasis). سازم is the 1st-person present (classical/poetic) form of ساختن/'to make/prepare' — 'I prepare/arm (myself)'. برهنه is an adjective 'bare/uncovered' placed before تنا (an older form of تن, 'torso/body'), so برهنه تنا = 'bare-bodied' (poetic word order).
segments.s243-g59: نه ... نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor'. شبرنگ is a noun meaning a variegated or patterned cloth/garment (poetic/archaic), and با من 'with me' follows it: 'no patterned robe with me'. رهوار is an old word for a traveller/wayfarer or rider; بور is an adjective 'fair/pale' placed after the noun (poetic adjective placement), so نه رهوار بور = 'nor a fair-haired wayfarer'.
segments.s243-g60: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/behold'. که here links the emphasis to the following statement (classical use, roughly 'for/that'), برگشتم is 1st-person past 'I returned' (برگشتن), امروز is the time adverb 'today', and هور is a vocative or name (here used as a proper name or address). The whole clause functions as an emphatic statement: 'truly, I returned today, Hoor.'
segments.s243-g61: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and here marks domain 'from the world' (ز گیتی = 'in/from the world'). نبینم is the 1st-person present-negative 'I do not see' (poetic order with verb before adverb sometimes), همی is an emphatic adverb 'ever/at all', یار is 'friend/companion', and کس is an indefinite 'anyone/person' — together expressing 'I see no friend/person in the world.'
segments.s243-g62: بجز means 'except/apart from'. ایزدم is ایزد + ـم (possessive) 'my god/deity' (classical form). نیست is the negated copula 'is not/does not exist', and فریادرس is a compound noun 'rescuer/succourer/helper'. Literal order is 'except my god there is no rescuer' = 'there is no helper except my god.'
segments.s243-g63: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. گیو و گودرز are proper names (Giv and Goudarz). کشوادگان is an archaic/title-like plural (poetic epithet) describing those men — the exact nuance is archaic but functions like 'champions/men of action' in apposition to the names.
segments.s243-g64: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. سر here is literal 'head' and in the idiom سر داد (سر + داد) means 'gave up the head' = 'surrendered one's life'; داد is the past 'gave'. باید is the modal 'must/ought to', همی is an emphatic particle here ('indeed/just'), and رایگان means 'free/for nothing'. Together the clause reads roughly 'who must give their heads freely' — i.e., 'who ought to (willingly) lay down their lives.'
segments.s243-g17: This short clause places the verb at the end (classical Persian order). خُونِ مژگان (خون + مژگان) is a genitive noun phrase 'the blood of the lashes' and از مژه marks the origin 'from the eyelash(s)'; برفت is the past-tense verb 'went/ran off/flowed' placed final. بدست appears as an initial adverbial/prepositional element (archaic/poetic placement) modifying the clause as 'by/through' or marking manner/agency.
segments.s243-g18: برآشفت is a past/stative verb meaning 'became angry/grew agitated' and functions as the main verb of this clause. و simply coordinates the next clause. این داستان باز گفت has the object (این داستان) before the verb (گفت) in typical Persian word order; باز 'again' modifies گفت to give 'told again' or 'repeated the tale.' Note: the supplied senses for گفت do not include the past-verb sense 'said/told', so that sense is signalled separately.
segments.s243-g19: کرا is an archaism equivalent to 'who/whoever' introducing an indefinite/relative clause. از پس پرده is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the curtain' (پس here = 'behind/after'); دختر is the noun 'maiden/maid' and بود is the past copula 'was' placed at the end as the clause's predicate.
segments.s243-g20: This is a conditional comment introduced by اگر 'if'. تاج دارد literally 'has a crown' (دارد present/persistent form), followed by بداختر بود where بداختر is an adjective 'ill‑starred/ill‑fated' and بود is the past copula; classical Persian often combines such tenses to state a general or reported conditional: 'if she had/has a crown, she was/is ill‑fated.'
segments.s243-g21: Again کرا serves as an archaic indefinite/conditional 'whoever/if', with the verb آید 'comes' (present/optative sense) placed after the subject دختر. بجای + noun is the standard construction meaning 'in the place of / instead of' so the clause reads 'if a daughter comes instead of a son.'
segments.s243-g22: This clause states the result or comparison. Classical 'به از' can introduce a comparative sense ('better that...'), here followed by a prepositional-from phrase از گور 'from the grave' and داماد 'groom' as the semantic subject/actor; ناید is the negative present/past-contrary form 'does not/should not come', and بدر is a noun placed final (here read as a lexical item rendered in the line). The overall construction yields a proverbial-sounding result: 'better that the groom not come from the grave.'
segments.s243-g23: ز (poetic variant of از) means 'because of' here, introducing the cause phrase ز کارِ منیژه 'because of Manijeh's deed' (کار منیژه is a genitive). دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his), خیره is a predicative adjective 'stunned/astonished', and ماند (past) 'remained' completes the predicate: 'his heart remained astonished' (i.e., he was stunned).
segments.s243-g24: قراخان سالار is a title + noun compound ('Qara Khan, the commander'); را is the definite object marker. پیش خواند is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'called before' or 'summoned' (پیش = 'in the presence of/before', خواند here = 'called/summoned'), with the verb in past tense at the end.
segments.s243-g121: This string gives the location and description: بدو 'to/unto him/it' is an archaic prepositional clitic meaning 'to/for him/it' placed before the rest; اندرون = 'inside/within' marks the interior location; خفته is the past participle/adjective 'sleeping'; بت پیکری is a compound noun phrase 'idol‑shaped/beautiful‑formed figure' (بت used poetically for a beautiful form). Word order is demonstrative/indirect object (بدو) → place (اندرون) → adjectival description (خفته) → head noun (بت پیکری).
segments.s243-g122: نهاده is the past participle/preterite 'placed/put' (perfective action). ببالین 'at the bedside/close beside' indicates the spatial relation of the placed object. برش here is an enclitic construction (بر + ش = 'on/over her' or 'upon it') effectively meaning 'upon her/it' (not the listed lexical senses); افسری is the noun 'diadem/ornament' placed there. The verb precedes its spatial complements in this poetic word order, and the clitic in برش attaches to show possession/target.
segments.s243-g123: پری is the supernatural noun 'fairy' and functions as the agent; (the missing یک in the word list is the numeral 'one/a'). بیک 'bey' is the person affected; ز introduces the object/source 'from/of' and links to اهرمن 'Ahriman/the devil' as the thing recalled; کرد + یاد is the causative construction 'made (him) remember' where کرد is the verb 'did/made' and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' used as the verbal object. Word order is agent → (object) → prepositional source → verb + object noun in a compressed, poetic sequence.
segments.s243-g124: میان سواران درآمد is the verbal phrase 'he entered among the horsemen' with میان marking 'among/amongst' and سواران 'horsemen' as the group; درآمد here is the perfective verb 'entered' (poetic past). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = like/as, باد = wind) modifying the manner of his entrance. Note the verb درآمد follows the locative phrase, a typical Persian order in poetry.
segments.s243-g125: This clause contains the object marker split (م + را in the provided word list) corresponding to مرا 'me' (object). ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly'; در = 'in/into' and عماری is a nominal 'dwelling/small house' (the place where I was set); نشاند is an archaic perfective verb 'sat/placed (someone)' (here 'set me down'). The overall structure is [object] + adverb + locative + verb, a common inversion in classical Persian.
segments.s243-g126: بران is an adverbial/connecting particle 'thereupon/then'; خوب‌چهره is an adjective 'fair‑faced/beautiful‑visaged' describing the woman who is the subject of the next verb; فسونی is a noun/adjective 'spell/enchanting' used here as 'a spell' or 'magic incantation'; بخواند is the past tense/preterite 'recited/uttered' (third person singular). The clause thus reads 'thereupon the fair‑faced one recited an enchantment.'
segments.s243-g127: که تا here introduces a result/temporal clause 'so that/until'; اندر = 'in/within' and ایوان = 'iwan/porch' specifying the place; نیامد is the negative past 'did not come' (here meaning 'had not come out of sleep'); ز خواب 'from sleep' gives the source/state from which she did not yet come. The clause sets a temporal boundary: she did not move until she came forth from sleep.
segments.s243-g128: نجنبید is the negative past 'did not stir/move' (here applied to the woman); و = 'and'; من = 'I' is the pronoun subject of the following verbless phrase; چشم کرده is a causative/perfective participial phrase literally 'having made the eye(s)' i.e. 'with eyes made' (common Persian idiom for 'with eyes filled'); پر آب is a prepositional nominal phrase 'full of water' meaning 'full of tears.' Together the segment reads 'and I, my eyes made full of water...' (leading into the next line).
segments.s243-g49: بزد دست is a past-tense action phrase literally 'he struck (with his) hand' where بزد is 3rd-person past 'struck' and دست is its instrument. و links the two verbs. برکند بندش ز جای is a compound: برکند (past 'dislodged/teared off') takes بندش ('its fastening/binding') as object, and ز جای ('from its place') is a prepositional phrase indicating source.
segments.s243-g50: بجست is past 'he leapt' (an intransitive perfective). از میان در is a sequence of prepositional elements 'out of/from the middle of the door' (از = from, میان = middle, در = door). اندر سرای 'into the hall/house' uses اندر as the preposition 'in/within' and سرای is the locative; the lemma sense list given doesn't match 'house' so the sense index is left unknown.
segments.s243-g51: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. بنزدیک is an adverbial locative 'near/close by' modifying the verb, followed by آن خانه 'that house' as its point of approach. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' indicating the manner or rapidity of his coming.
segments.s243-g52: This clause gives the reason/background: کجا functions here as 'where' (introducing the location/fact). پیشگه is an adverb 'previously/beforehand'. مرد بیگانه بود is a simple past copular clause 'there was a strange/foreign man' (مرد = man; بیگانه = stranger; بود = was).
segments.s243-g53: This is a temporal/subordinate clause: ز در 'from the door' gives the source, چون functions as a conjunction 'when/as', and به بیژن indicates direction 'toward Bijan'. برافگند is a past verb 'threw/cast' and چشم (here object) means 'his gaze/eye' — together 'when he cast his gaze from the door toward Bijan.' Word order is Poetic: adverbials and conjunction precede the verb and its object.
segments.s243-g54: بچوشید is past intransitive 'it gushed/welled' used idiomatically with خونش ('his blood') as subject/possessor => 'his blood gushed/welled up'. برگ بر ز خشم is a short prepositional phrase where بر is the preposition 'upon/over' and ز خشم 'from/with anger' gives the cause. (Note: برگ appears as a lexical item here but in the supplied sense list its usual senses don't match the poetic/metaphorical use; the clause as a whole expresses a physiological/affective reaction—blood surging and anger.)
segments.s243-g55: در آن خانه is the locative prepositional phrase 'in that house'. سیصد is the numeral 'three hundred' modifying پرستنده ('worshippers/devotees'), and بود is the past copula 'there were'. The whole is a simple past locative-existential clause.
segments.s243-g56: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject modifier, با introduces accompaniment 'with', followed by a list: رباب (a bowed string instrument), نبید (wine/must), and سرود (song/ode). The conjunction و links the list items; the phrase describes how the worshippers were gathered—with instruments, drink, and song.
segments.s243-g185: بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial particle meaning 'before/in the presence of' and precedes the noun phrase نیاکان. پهلو منش is a two‑word descriptive group ('پهلو' = side/lineage + 'منش' = origin) that qualifies نیاکان as 'kinsmen/ancestors of the same line'; the order (preposition + noun + modifier) is typical of older Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s243-g186: پس از مرگ is a temporal prepositional phrase ('پس' = after + 'از' = of/from) meaning 'after death'. The clause uses an impersonal/affected construction: بر من بود سرزنش literally 'reproach was upon me' where بود is the past copula ('was') and سرزنش is the predicate noun; بر من marks the experiencer ('against me').
segments.s243-g187: روانم is the possessed noun 'my soul' (روان + -م). بماند is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'may remain' or 'let ... remain' expressing a wish; هم ایدر بجای is an adverbial cluster ('also, here, in this place') giving the location/emphasis after the verb, a common poetic word order.
segments.s243-g188: ز شرم پدر is an ablative/cause phrase 'because of/for the shame of my father' (ز = because of). چون introduces an interrogative 'how' (چون = how) and شوم is the first‑person subjunctive/optative 'shall I become / can I become' (here 'can I return'); باز جای 'باز' = again and 'جای' = place form the verbal complement 'return to my place' (literally 'become again place').
segments.s243-g189: دریغا is an interjection 'Alas!'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. شادان شود is 'become joyful' (شود here is a verb of becoming, with a potential/subjunctive sense) and دشمنم is the subject with possessive suffix ('my enemy' or 'my foes'); word order places the verb before its subject, which is common in classical verse.
segments.s243-g190: چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. بینند is 3rd‑person plural present/optative 'they see' (or 'should see'). بر دار = 'on the gallows' (بر = on, دار here = gallows). روشن qualifies تنم ('my body') as 'bright/illuminated'; تنم is the possessed noun with the suffix -م. The predicate sequence is temporal clause → verb → locative phrase → noun phrase.
segments.s243-g191: دریغا opens with lamentation. ز شاه و ز مردان is a pair of prepositional phrases 'from the king and from the men'; نیو (ney, the reed‑flute) stands nominally and implies the sound of mourning (the verb is omitted, a common poetic ellipsis: 'there will be the ney/from them will come the ney').
segments.s243-g192: Again دریغا introduces the exclamation 'Alas that ...'. که introduces the clause. دورم is a verbless predicate 'I am far/distant' (دور + -م). ز دیدار گیو is an ablative phrase 'from the sight/meeting of Giv' (Giv is a proper name), so the whole clause means 'Alas that I am far from seeing Giv.'
segments.s243-g65: This fragment is a locative/quantifying phrase. همیشه is an adverb 'always'. بیک here stands for an older contracted phrase meaning 'به یک' ('in/inside one' — i.e. 'in a single'), an archaic form not listed in the glosses. ساق موزه درون is a prepositional stack meaning 'in (the) sheath/by his side' (درون = 'inside'). Word order is typical Persian: adverb → locative phrase → inside-marker. Several nouns here are archaic compounds and the glosses supplied do not match exactly.
segments.s243-g66: This is the main possessed‑phrase: یکی خنجری داشتی آبگون = 'he had one dagger, bluish'. یکی = 'one/a certain', خنجری is the noun 'a dagger' (indefinite), داشتی is the past possessive here (archaic/palatalized past form meaning 'he had'), and آبگون is an adjectival postmodifier 'bluish'. The adjective follows the noun as in Persian.
segments.s243-g67: This clause contains two coordinated verbal actions. بزد دست = 'he struck (with) his hand' — بزد is past 'he struck', دست is the instrument (hand). و links to خنجر کشید از نیام = 'and (he) drew the dagger from the sheath'; کشید is the past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw' used here with خنجر as object, and از + نیام marks origin 'out of the scabbard'. The verb order (verb after object/instrument) is normal for classical Persian narration.
segments.s243-g68: در خانه بگرفت و برگفت نام is two short past actions joined by و. در + (noun) gives a locative phrase; بگرفت is the past 'he seized / took hold (of it)'. برگفت here means 'he spoke/uttered' and نام is the object 'the name' (i.e. he called out his name). Note: خانه in this line functions idiomatically (context implies 'in his hand / in his grasp' or 'thereupon'); that idiomatic sense is archaic and not the literal 'house' listed in the glosses.
segments.s243-g69: This is the beginning of a reported quotation introduced by که 'that'. من بیژنم = 'I am Bijan' (بیژنم is the proper name Bijan in the nominative position). پور + [name] is the patronymic construction 'son of …' (پور = 'son'). The sequence is standard: complementizer که → pronoun subject → name → patronymic.
segments.s243-g70: سر پهلوانان و آزادگان functions as a predicative or appositive title: سر here means 'chief/leader' (not physical head), followed by the genitive plural پهلوانان 'of the champions' and و آزادگان 'and of the free/noble (men)'. The noun سر heads the nominal phrase and governs the following plurals.
segments.s243-g71: This negative clause starts with ندرد ('does not / will not tear') used generically: with کسی (here functioning in a negative context = 'no one') it yields 'no one tears...'. پوست بر من = 'the skin upon me' (بر = on/upon, denoting the surface affected). مگر introduces the exception/conditional ('unless' / 'except that') which links to the result clause that follows.
segments.s243-g72: The result clause: همی is an emphatic particle ('then/at once' or 'indeed'), سیری آید = 'a [thing/event] comes' (آید = 'comes' in present/future sense); تنش را ز سر = 'his body (تنش) from the head' with را marking the object. Pragmatically it means 'unless immediately his body is separated from his head.' Note that سیری is an old or context‑specific noun here (the supplied glosses do not give a precise match), so I render the sense as a resultant event coming about.
segments.s243-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. بگذشت is the past-tense verb 'passed' (time passed). یک چندگاه is an idiomatic duration 'a while' (literally 'one some-time'), and این‌چنین 'thus/like this' modifies the whole temporal clause; word order (چو + verb + duration + manner) is the literary/poetic inversion.
segments.s243-g2: پس is a discourse adverb 'then'. آگاهی آمد is a light construction where the noun 'information/news' (آگاهی) is followed by آمد 'arrived/came'. بدربان is the dative-like expression 'to the gatekeeper' (ب- prefix + دربان). ازین is from/of this (از + این written together); it marks the content of the news.
segments.s243-g3: نهفته is an adjective 'hidden' qualifying همه کارشان 'all their affairs' (literal order: adjective + noun phrase). بازجست is a past/preterite verb 'sought/searched out' in an older/poetic form; the clause therefore means 'he searched out all their hidden affairs.'
segments.s243-g4: بژرفی is an adverb 'deeply/profoundly' (ب- + ژرفی). نگه کرد is a compound of the noun نگه 'watch/gaze' plus the past verb کرد 'did/made', here functioning as 'he cast a close look / examined'. کار 'the affair' is the object, and از نخست 'from the first' (از = from; نخست = initially) indicates that his scrutiny began at the start.
segments.s243-g5: کسی introduces a general indefinite 'someone/whoever'. کز is a literary contraction of 'که (از)' used as a relative 'who/that'. گزافه سخن is a noun phrase 'vain/exaggerated speech'. راندا is an archaic/poetic verbal form (a participial/agentive shape) meaning 'who utters / sets forth' (here functioning within the relative clause).
segments.s243-g6: درخت بلا (with را marking it as the definite object) is 'the tree of calamity'. بجنباندا is an older/poetic verb form (built from بجنباند 'to set in motion; to shake') meaning 'causes to move / shakes' and serves as the main verb of the relative clause; the object marker را shows that درخت بلا is what is set in motion.
segments.s243-g7: نگه کرد is 'he looked/observed' (noun نگه 'gaze' + past کرد 'did' used as a verbal phrase meaning 'he examined/asked'). کو and کی are interrogative words in poetic word order (کو = where, کی = who) followed by the copula ست 'is'; شهرش کجاست is the paired question 'and where is his city?' The inversion and repetition of copula are typical of literary Persian questions.
segments.s243-g8: بدین is an old literary form meaning 'to/for this' (here read as ب- + این = 'to this/for this'); آمدن is the infinitive/masdar 'coming' used with بدین to indicate 'his coming'. سوی توران is a prepositional phrase 'toward Turan'. چراست is a fixed interrogative/explanatory phrase 'why is it?' i.e. 'what is the reason?' — together asking why he came toward Turan.
segments.s243-g161: بگرسیوز is an uncertain/poetic/regional verb form (given as an unanalyzed item here) likely functioning as an imperative or exhortation ('turn' / 'look round'); اندر یکی is a prepositional phrase ('in/within' + 'one/once') that gives the locus or single act of looking; بنگرید is a classical present/subjunctive/imperative form of نگر (to look) in the polite/2nd-person-plural register, here commanding or urging the addressee(s) to 'look'. The clause order places the exhortation verb first, then the locative, then the main verb for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s243-g162: کز is the archaic/poetic variant of از and here means 'of/from' (marking subject matter: 'of Iran'); ایران is the place/topic 'Iran'; چه is the interrogative/relative 'what' introducing the content to be looked at; دیدیم is the 1st‑person plural past 'we saw'; و is the coordinating 'and'; خواهیم is the auxiliary 'we will' (future); دید is the infinitive/root 'see' so خواهیم دید = 'we will see'. The clause therefore reports both past and future sight of Iran.
segments.s243-g163: نبینی is the negative present/optative form of دید (classical Persian), here functioning rhetorically as 'do you not see' or 'you do not see'; که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'; این 'this' points to the person; بدکنش is a compound noun 'bad‑doer / ill‑behaved one'; ریمنا is a proper name. The clause is a rhetorical question introducing what follows.
segments.s243-g164: فزونی is a noun meaning 'increase' or 'growing intensity'; سگالد is a verbal/nominative form referring to the act of 'setting dogs on / harrying' (here treated as the hostile action); همی is the habitual/continuous adverb 'always / evermore' marking repeated action; بر is the preposition 'upon/against'; منا is an older/poetic form referring to 'us' (in context 'against us'). Together the sequence says that the harrying with dogs increases against us.
segments.s243-g165: بسنده 'sufficient' (adjective/adverb) begins the clause; نبودش is the past negative 'was not' with the clitic pronoun -ش 'it/him', so 'it was not sufficient'; همین 'this/just this' and بد 'evil/bad deed' specify the action—که کرد 'that he did' is a relative clause (که + past verb) modifying the preceding. Word order places the evaluative phrase first and then the specifying relative clause.
segments.s243-g166: همی is the continuative adverb 'still/always' marking ongoing action; رزم 'battle/war' is the direct object of جوید, which is a present form of جست/جو meaning 'seeks'—so 'he seeks battle'; بننگ 'shame/disgrace' and نبرد 'combat' are additional nouns joined by و 'and', listing what he seeks. The verb جوید governs the sequence of nouns (rhematically: he seeks war, shame, and combat).
segments.s243-g167: ببر here is the imperative form (given among senses also as the animal 'tiger', but in context it functions as the verb imperative 'take' / 'bind'); همچنین 'likewise/similarly' modifies the command; بند is 'fetter/bond' (the object to be applied); بر دست و پای is the prepositional phrase 'on (his) hands and feet'—the order places the instrument/thing (بند) before the locative phrase. The clause is an imperative instruction to bind.
segments.s243-g168: هم 'also' + اندر زمان 'at the same time / in the moment' gives the temporal frame; ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here part of the phrase 'زو' = از او / from/away from there/him); (the separate و in the supplied token list is orthographic here) بپرداز is an imperative of پرداختن or پِرداختن meaning 'set/put/lead' (i.e. 'take/put him'); جای is the noun 'place' functioning as the destination. Together the clause orders that he be removed/led away and placed elsewhere.
segments.s243-g137: 'تو' is the 2nd-person pronoun addressing 'you'. 'آنی' here functions predicatively — an archaic/poetic word meaning 'the one (such as)' or 'such a one', linking the pronoun to the relative clause that follows. 'کز' is a relative particle 'who/that', introducing the clause 'from Iran with sword and lasso'; 'ایران' names the country; 'بتیغ' is the prepositional use of بِ + 'تیغ' meaning 'with sword'; 'و' coordinates 'کمند' (lasso/snare). Word order places the relative clause after the predicative 'آنی'.
segments.s243-g138: 'همی' is the old epic/prosodic auxiliary indicating ongoing or habitual action (here: continuous/repeated past sense). 'رزم جستی' is a compound where 'رزم' (battle) and the verb 'جستن' (here 'جستی' 2nd person form) mean 'you took up battle / you waged war' (in classical Persian 'رزم جستن' = to fight). 'به' introduces the purposive phrase 'به نام بلند' meaning 'for/to gain'; 'نام' is 'reputation/fame' and 'بلند' is 'lofty'. The verb form 'جستی' is 2nd-person singular, giving the whole clause: 'you have fought/waged war (repeatedly) for high renown.'
segments.s243-g139: 'کنون' is a temporal adverb 'now'. 'چون زنان' is a simile 'like women' (چون = like). 'پیش من' means 'in my presence / before me' (پیش + personal pronoun). 'بسته دست' is a participial phrase 'with (your) hands bound' where the adjective/participle 'بسته' precedes the noun 'دست' giving a circumstantial description of the person standing before the speaker.
segments.s243-g140: 'همی' again signals continued/habitual action or an archaic narrative present. 'خواب گویی' here is a verbal phrase meaning 'you seem/as if (in) sleep' or 'you speak/act as if asleep' — 'گویی' is used to indicate appearing/uttering (an archaic use of the verb form). 'به کردار مست' is an adverbial phrase formed with 'به' ('in/by') + 'کردار' (behavior/conduct) + 'مست' (intoxicated/drunk, here figurative), so 'in the behavior of a drunk' = 'acting like a drunk.'
segments.s243-g141: 'بکار' here is the verb/adverbial element meaning 'to put into use / to employ' (used with an infinitive), so 'بکار دروغ آزمودن' means 'to put lies to the test / to try using lies'. 'دروغ' is 'lie', 'آزمودن' is the infinitive 'to test/try', and 'همی' marks habitual or ongoing action: 'you are always trying/testing lies.'
segments.s243-g142: 'بخواهی' is the 2nd-person present/optative of 'خواستن' meaning 'you wish/you would try' and here governs the following infinitive. 'سر از من ربودن' is an infinitival phrase 'to snatch the head away from me' where 'سر' (head) is the object, 'از من' expresses separation 'from me', and 'ربودن' is the infinitive 'to snatch/steal/take away'. The final 'همی' again gives a sense of repeated/ongoing intent. Overall: 'you try (or would try) to snatch my head away.'
segments.s243-g143: 'بدو' is the clitic prepositional expression 'to him' indicating direction of the speech. 'گفت' is the past tense verb 'said' (here reported speech). 'بیژن' is the speaker's name. 'که‌ای شهریار' is an archaic vocative construction: 'که‌ای' (equivalent to 'که ای' / 'O') + 'شهریار' (king), so 'he said to him: "O king..."' — the sequence places the vocative after the reporting clause.
segments.s243-g144: 'سخن بشنو' is an imperative phrase: 'hear a word' or 'listen to what I say' ('بشنو' is 2nd-person imperative of 'شنیدن'). 'از من' marks source 'from me'. 'یکی هوشیار' is an appositive or self-description: 'one (a certain) who is alert' — 'یکی' = 'a certain/one' and 'هوشیار' = 'alert/watchful', so the speaker urges the king to heed the words of an alert man (i.e., himself).
segments.s243-g97: چو آمد بنزدیک شاه اندرا is a temporal clause. چو here is the subordinating particle 'when'; آمد is the past form 'came'; بنزدیک ('near') follows the verb in this inverted, poetic word order; شاه اندرا is a title + name in apposition ('King Andra').
segments.s243-g98: گو is a narrative particle meaning 'saying' or 'he said/crying', introducing the words or description that follow; دست‌بسته and برهنه are coordinate adjectives describing state ('hands-bound, naked'); سرا here means 'hall'/'house' (the place into/at which the cry or description is directed).
segments.s243-g99: برو is an imperative 'go' (an address or command). آفرین here functions as an address/exclamation 'praiser' or 'one who utters praise' and is used vocatively; کردکای شهریار is a name or epithet (proper-name/epithet + 'sovereign') used in apposition as the addressee.
segments.s243-g100: گر introduces a conditional 'if'; از من + کنی is 'if (you) do/from me' with کنی the present/subjunctive form of کردن 'do/seek' ('if you seek/ask of me'); راستی is the noun 'truth'; خواستار is an adjectival/nominal participle 'seeker/desirer' — together 'if you are/if you ask me for truth.'
segments.s243-g101: بگویم is a first-person verbal form meaning 'I will tell' or 'let me tell' (optative/volitive use in narrative). ترا is the enclitic object 'you' (here written as ت + را), را is the definite/object marker attached to the following noun, سربسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely', and داستان is 'story/tale' — together: 'I will tell you the whole story.'
segments.s243-g102: چو again introduces a conditional/temporal 'when/if'; گردی here is a verb-form meaning roughly 'you become/turn' (archaic/poetic usage; the listed lexical senses did not match this verbal sense); بگفتار is a prepositional/adverbial compound 'in/according to speech' (i.e. 'in words' or 'with respect to what is said'); همداستان is an adjective 'of the same mind/like‑minded' — together 'if/when you, in words, are of the same mind' (i.e. 'if you agree with my account').
segments.s243-g103: نه negates the clause 'not'; من is the subject 'I'; بآزرو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'with desire / out of desire' (ب- + آرزو/آزرو); جستم is the past form 'I sought/came' (here 'I came/attended'); این is 'this'; جشنگاه is 'place/occasion of festivity' — overall: 'I did not come to this festival out of desire.'
segments.s243-g104: نبود is the negative past/existential 'there was not/was no'; اندرین کار is a prepositional phrase 'in this matter/affair' (اندرین = 'in this'); کس را گناه literally 'blame to any person' where را marks the object and گناه means 'fault/blame' — the clause asserts 'in this matter there was no one's fault.'
segments.s243-g41: A nominal phrase meaning “the wailing/crying of the harp and (the) cry of the rebab.” غریویدن is a verbal-noun/infinitive used like a noun ‘wailing/crying’; the two instrument-phrases are joined by و without an explicit ezâfe, a typical poetic ellipsis (i.e. بانگِ رباب).
segments.s243-g42: برآمد is a past/telic verb here meaning “arose/issued forth.” ز (poetic form of از) marks the source 'from', followed by ایوان افراسیاب (the iwan/porch belonging to Afrasiab), where the possessor follows the noun in this poetic word-order.
segments.s243-g43: A locative noun phrase: سواران (horsemen) + places they occupy در و بام (gates/doors and rooftops). آن کاخ شاه = 'that palace of the king' (poetic order, ezâfe omitted). در here means 'door/gate' rather than the preposition.
segments.s243-g44: Two coordinated past verbs: گرفتند (they seized/occupied) and ببستند (they shut/blocked). هر سو ('every side' / 'in all directions') is an adverbial phrase modifying ببستند; راه is the object 'the way/road' that they closed off.
segments.s243-g45: چو (here temporal: 'when') introduces the subordinate clause. The following token corresponds to the person who perceives (here the name Garsivaz in the poem); then آن کاخ در بسته دید = 'he saw that palace's door closed' with درِ بسته functioning as a predicate/adjectival phrase. Classical poetic style often omits the linking ezâfe.
segments.s243-g46: می‌و غلغل نوش is a coordinated noun phrase meaning 'wine and the clamor/uproar of drinking/revelry' (غلغل modifies نوش), پیوسته is an adverb 'continually/unceasingly', and دید is the past verb 'he saw.' Word order places the objects before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s243-g47: سواران is subject 'the horsemen.' گرفتندگرد is a compound/phasally-joined action meaning 'they surrounded/closed in' (گرفتند + گرد 'around'); اندرش is a prepositional/locative 'in/around him' formed with اندر + the enclitic -ش ('his/him'). This is past-tense plural action.
segments.s243-g48: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as.' سالار شد is a past clause 'the leader turned/acted as leader' (شد = happened/became); سوی is the preposition 'toward,' and بسته درش is a noun phrase 'his closed door' (بسته = closed; در = door; -ش = his). The phrase 'سوی بسته درش' is the directional complement 'toward his closed door.'
segments.s243-g89: وفا کرد is a noun + کردن construction meaning 'to perform/keep fidelity' (کرد is the past verb). با او marks the beneficiary/target ('to/with him'). بسوگندها is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'by oaths' (ب‑ + سوگندها) placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s243-g90: بخوبی is an adverb 'well/properly' modifying the following verb; بدادش is the past verb 'داد' with an attached indirect object pronoun ‑ش ('to him') — here it functions as 'gave him' (the listed lemma senses do not include 'give', so sense is marked unavailable). بسی quantifies پندها 'many/much advice' (in poetry the quantifier follows the verb cluster).
segments.s243-g91: بپیمان is an adverbial/particle meaning roughly 'by (a) pledge' (an older contracted form). جدا کرد is 'separated/removed' (کرد past); ز + و here stand for زِ او (زو) 'from him' and the final خنجرا 'the dagger' is the thing removed — classical Persian often contracts preposition + pronoun (زو) and places object after the verb.
segments.s243-g92: بخوبی again adverb 'well'; کشیدش is past 'کشید' with enclitic ‑ش 'him' ('he dragged/pulled him'); ببند is an imperative 'bind/fasten' addressed to the named person, and اندرا is the vocative/name (Andra/Indra).
segments.s243-g93: بیاورد is the narrative past 'he brought'. بسته and بکردار are adjectival/postpositive modifiers ('bound', 'of good conduct') that describe the following noun یوز; poetry often places modifiers before the noun after the verb, so 'he brought (the) bound, well‑conducted Yuz.'
segments.s243-g94: چه سود introduces a rhetorical question 'what profit/use'; از هنرها is the genitive 'from the arts/skills'; چو برگشت روز is a temporal subordinate 'when the day returned' (چو = 'when'), so the clause asks about the value of skills once day/morning comes back.
segments.s243-g95: چنینست is a copular/existential 'it is thus/so'; کردار is the noun 'deed/conduct' and این گوژپشت is the noun phrase 'this hunchback' that the predicate refers to — word order is inverted for emphasis ('Such is the conduct of this hunchback').
segments.s243-g96: چو here means 'if/when'; نرمی 'softness/gentleness' is the subject of the next predicate بسودی (from 'سودیدن') 'were to avail'; بیابی is a second‑person subjunctive/formal clause 'you would find' (here a grammatical subjunctive/optative form), and درشت is the adjective 'coarse/rough' — the object 'him' (the hunchback) is implied.
segments.s243-g153: "اگر" introduces a conditional clause ('if'); "شاه" is the subject 'the king'; "خواهد" is the third-person present/future of خواستن used here as 'wants/wishes'; "که" introduces the subordinate clause; "بیند" is the present/subjunctive form of دیدن 'to see' (the king seeing); and "ز من" is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from/by me' indicating the agent of what is to be seen.
segments.s243-g154: "دلیری نمودن" is a light-noun + verb combination meaning 'to show/display bravery' (دلیری as the quality, نمودن the action). "بدین" here functions as به + این 'to/at this' (poetically written together) and modifies "انجمن"; "انجمن" is the place "assembly/meeting" — together 'to show bravery in this assembly'.
segments.s243-g155: "یکی اسب" = 'one (a) horse' with numerical "یکی" before the noun; "فرمای" is an archaic/polite verb form used here as a command/ordering ('command/bring' or 'order that one bring'); "و گرزی گران" coordinates a second item 'and a heavy mace' (گرزی = mace, گران = heavy/weighty).
segments.s243-g156: The verb phrase "گزین کن" is an imperative 'choose/select' (گزین + کن as the imperative construction). "ز ترکان" ('from the Turks') marks the source, and the object is "هزار از سران" = 'a thousand of the chiefs/ leaders' (هزار = a thousand, سران = leaders). Word order is poetic but means 'select a thousand leaders from among the Turks.'
segments.s243-g157: به + آوردگه is a prepositional phrase 'to the آوردگه' (arena/place of arrival). بر introduces the location 'on/upon' and governs یکی 'one' followed by زین 'saddle' with هزار 'thousand' understood as a group: the word order is poetic/elliptical for 'on one of these thousand saddles' (i.e. put me on one saddle among a thousand).
segments.s243-g158: اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. زنده مانم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive sense 'I remain alive'. بمردم is the root meaning 'I die' (here part of the plea/request). مدار here functions as an imperative/optative particle in the clause (classical/poetic usage not matched by the given noun senses), so the whole line reads as an entreaty: 'If I remain alive, (do) not cause me to die.'
segments.s243-g159: ز (poetic 'from') + بیژن with چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause: 'when Bijan heard these words'. گفته (the participle/noun 'the said/these words') is object of بشنید 'he heard'. چشم 'eye' here names the thing that reacts (his gaze) and serves as the subject of the following verbal action.
segments.s243-g160: بروبر is an adverbial/particle meaning 'directly/face-to-face' modifying فگند, the archaic past form of فکند 'threw' (here 'cast a look'). The coordination و links this clause to برآورد خشم, where برآورد is past of برآوردن 'brought forth/caused' and خشم is the object 'anger' — 'and he brought forth/raised anger' (i.e. became angry).
segments.s243-g25: بدو گفت is a reversed, literary order for به او گفت (‘he said to him’): بدو = به + او (dative). ازین is an orthographic contraction of از این (= دربارهٔ این / concerning this); کار ناپاک is noun + adjective (the ‘immoral deed/affair’), and زن follows to specify whose deed it is (‘the woman’s’).
segments.s243-g26: هشیوار here is used as an exhortation/adjective meaning ‘be alert/wise’ (poetic imperative sense). با من = ‘with me’ (company/in consultation). یکی رای = ‘one counsel/one plan’ (یکی as numeral/indefinite + رای = counsel). زن repeats the referent ‘the woman’ (i.e. the plan concerns the woman); the whole clause = ‘be alert — take one counsel with me about the woman.’
segments.s243-g27: قراخان is the subject (proper name/title). چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’. داد is the past tense verb ‘gave’ used with پاسخ (دادِ پاسخ = ‘gave an answer’ = ‘replied’). بشاه is the dative form (ب + شاه) ‘to the king’.
segments.s243-g28: که introduces the content of the reply (‘that’). در کار = ‘in the matter/affair’ (در + کار). هشیارتر is the comparative adjective ‘more alert/more vigilant’. کن is the imperative ‘do/make’ and نگاه (normally a noun) is used here in a verblike imperative sense (‘look/watch’); the word order is poetic (هشیارتر کن نگاه rather than نگاه کن هشیارتر).
segments.s243-g29: اگر هست = ‘if it exists/is so’ (conditional). خود is an intensifier/reflexive here (‘itself/the thing itself’). جای گفتار نیست is an idiomatic negation: جای = ‘place/room’, گفتار = ‘speech/talk’, نیست = ‘is not’ → ‘there is no place for talk.’
segments.s243-g30: ولیکن = ‘but/yet’. شنیدن is the verbal noun/gerund ‘hearing/being told’. چو = ‘as/like’. دیدار here functions as ‘seeing’/encounter (poetic use of دیدار for sight/meeting). نیست = ‘is not’ → ‘hearing is not like seeing.’
segments.s243-g31: بگرسیوز is a proper/name or a dialectal form (appears as a name or irregular verb form in the text). آنگاه = ‘then/thereupon’. گفتش = گفت + ش (the clitic ش = ‘to him/her’) = ‘said to him’. بدرد functions predicatively here (بد + در) meaning ‘of use’ / ‘useful’ (in context it reports his utterance: ‘it will be of use’ or ‘that avails’).
segments.s243-g32: پر از = ‘full of’ (پر followed by از + noun). خون دل is a compound ‘heart’s blood’ (خون + دل = deep grief/bleeding heart). و = ‘and’. دیده پر آب = ‘eyes full of water’ (i.e. full of tears). زرد is an adjective after the noun phrase meaning ‘yellow/pale’ (poetic color image: ‘yellow/pale tears’). The whole is a descriptive clause of his sorrowful state.
segments.s243-g81: This phrase functions as the protasis ('if' clause) of a conditional. گرم here appears at the start as a conditional marker (classical/poetic usage of 'گر' + enclitic) rather than the literal adjective 'warm'; نزد سالار توران is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / to the commander of Turan' (نزد before the noun), with سالار (commander) as head and توران as its genitive/identifier. بری at the end would be expected as a verbal element meaning 'you go' in colloquial/poetic form, but the provided lexical senses for بری do not match its verbal use here.
segments.s243-g82: بخوبی (adverb) modifies the following imperative برو ('go' / 'proceed') and qualifies the manner 'well/thoroughly'; داستان is the direct object 'the tale/story' and آوری is a present/subjunctive form of آوردن used here in the sense 'relate / bring (as a report)'. Word order is adverb + imperative + object + verb (poetic), with the imperative asking that the addressee bring/relate the tale well.
segments.s243-g83: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. خواهشگری (a deverbal noun) plus the imperative کن = 'perform a pleading / make an entreaty' (i.e. 'entreat'). مـرا/را is the object clitic sequence ('مرا' = 'me', split in the gloss), with ز + او (here given as separate tokens ز and و) meaning 'from him' or 'about him' (ز = 'from/of'; و in the text functions as the pronoun 'او' = 'him'). بخون is an imperative 'recite / tell'. Note that the provided lemmas for م and و do not match their function here as clitics/pronouns, so they are treated as clitic object/pronoun rather than the senses listed.
segments.s243-g84: This short clause gives a conditional/concessive instruction: سزد‌گر (sazdgar) = 'if it is fitting' or 'if it seems right' (agentive/qualific sense used as a concessive marker), بنیکی = 'with goodness / kindly' (benign manner), بوی is an archaic contraction of 'به وی' = 'to him' / 'for him' (here functioning as a dative), and رهنمون = 'guide / guidance' (predicate noun). Together it means 'if it is fitting, with kindness be his guide.' Note poetic contractions and inverted order (manner + dative + role).
segments.s243-g85: نکرد is the past negative (did not do/made not), placed before the rest for emphasis (classical word order). ایچ = 'nothing / not at all' (negation intensifier), modifying the following noun; گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz'. آهنگ اوی = 'his intention/aim' with آهنگ as 'intention/aim' and اوی the possessive 'his'. The clause thus reads 'Garsivaz made no intention toward him' (i.e. he made no move/plan).
segments.s243-g86: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; دید is the past 'saw / perceived'; آن‌چنان is a demonstrative/adverb 'so / in that manner / such'; تیزی is 'keenness / swiftness / fierceness' and qualifies چنگ (here 'claw' / 'talon' or metaphorically 'hand/griffin-like blow'); اوی is again the possessive 'his'. The clause means 'when he saw how fierce/sharp his talon/hand was.'
segments.s243-g87: بدانست is the past 'he knew / he perceived'; کو here functions as a relative/particle 'who / that' in classical Persian (not simply 'where'), introducing the subordinate clause; راست means 'true' and modifies گوید (classical present/stative 'speaks / says'); همی is a classical imperfective/continuous marker 'always / ever'. The whole segment reads 'he knew who always spoke the truth.'
segments.s243-g88: This clause gives the factual consequence: بخون here is to be read as the fused preposition + noun 'با + خون' or simply 'in/with blood' (classical contraction b- + khun), followed by ریختن 'to pour / to shed' forming 'the shedding of blood'; دست 'hands'; شوید (from شدن) is the present/past-subjunctive/infinitival 'become / be made' and همی again adds continuous/epic aspect. Grammatically it says 'that by the shedding of blood the hands would become (i.e. be stained).' Note classical word order and contracted prepositional form.
segments.s243-g113: از + اسبم + جدا کرد is a short clause meaning '(he) separated from my horse' — here ‫جدا کرد‬ (past) is a transitive/perfective verb 'made separate / dismounted'. و شد is a coordinating sequence 'and (then) he became/was' and تا براه is an old-fashioned contracted phrase (تا = toward/as far as; براه = به راه) meaning 'toward the road / set off on the road'.
segments.s243-g114: که introduces a temporal/subordinate clause 'when/that'; آمد here is simple past 'came' and همی is the epic-era continuous marker giving an ongoing sense ('were coming'). لشکر و دختِ شاه is a coordinated noun phrase 'the army and the king's daughter' (دخت = daughter/of the king).
segments.s243-g115: سواران 'riders' is the head noun, followed by the postpositive adjective پراگنده 'scattered'. بر گرد دشت is a prepositional phrase 'around the plain' (بر + گرد = around; دشت = plain). Word order is typical Persian noun + modifier + locative phrase.
segments.s243-g116: چه مایه is an exclamative phrase 'what an amount / what a quantity'; عماری here means 'buildings / dwellings' (objects being noticed), بمن is dative 'to/for me' (here 'before me' / 'I witnessed'), and برگذشت (past) means 'passed by / came before' — so the whole line expresses astonished observation of many dwellings passing before the speaker.
segments.s243-g117: یکی is indefinite 'one / a certain (one)'; چتر هندی is a compound 'Indian canopy/umbrella' with the adjective هندی placed after the noun. برآمد is past 'arose/appeared', and ز دور (poetic for از دور) means 'from a distance.' The clause describes a single canopy showing up at a distance.
segments.s243-g118: ز هر سو 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every direction/side). گرفته is a perfect participle 'having taken/seized' or 'occupied', here describing the state of the plain, and سواران تور 'the Tur riders' (تور is the proper name/group) is the subject of that participial description — i.e. the Tur riders had come from every side / occupied the area.
segments.s243-g119: یکی کرده = 'one had made/placed' (کرده is a past participle / perfect 'made'). از عود مهدی میان literally 'of (a) wood/beam of Mahdi in the middle' — عود here is a piece of fragrant/valuable wood (or a wooden staff/pole), مهدی is the owner's/name, and میان 'in the middle' indicates position (a central pole placed in the middle).
segments.s243-g120: کشیده is the past participle 'drawn/stretched'; برو here is an archaic/poetic locative/relational particle (functioning like بر / 'over/on it') rather than the literal verb 'go' — so کشیده برو چادر پرنیان = 'a silken tent (چادر پرنیان) drawn/stretched over it.' پرنیان is an adjective/noun 'silken' or 'silken robe', qualifying the tent.
segments.s243-g129: This is a compact classical negation: گناهی (’any sin’) + مرا (me, accusative; here split in the word list into م + را) + اندرین (‘in this’) + بوده نیست (a periphrastic past negative: been‑not). Together it says that no sin of mine has existed in this matter. Note the word order places the indefinite noun گناهی up front for emphasis and uses بوده نیست instead of a single negative verb.
segments.s243-g130: منیژه is the subject (a proper name). بدین کار is a prepositional phrase (بدین = به + این, ‘with/at this’; کار = ‘deed, affair’) and آلوده نیست is the predicate adjective with negation (‘is not tainted’). The negative copula نیست denies that Manijeh is morally or otherwise implicated.
segments.s243-g131: پری (‘a fairy’) is the noun subject, modified by the adverb بی‌گمان (‘without doubt’). بخت‌برگشته is an adjectival compound meaning ‘ill‑fated/unlucky’, and بود is the past copula (‘was’): the clause states that, without doubt, an ill‑fated fairy was present (or had come).
segments.s243-g132: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause (‘who/that’). بر من = ‘upon me’ (prepositional object). همی is an archaic aspectual particle used with verbs in Ferdowsi to indicate ongoing/habitual or simple past aspect before the verb. جادوی is the object ‘magic/sorcery’ and آزمود is the past tense verb ‘tried/tested’. Word order (object before verb) is normal in older Persian poetry.
segments.s243-g133: چنین بد (‘such/so bad’) begins the clause meaning ‘as I said’ or ‘such as I described’. که links to reported speech or explanation (‘that/which’), گفتم is ‘I said’. کم و بیش is the idiom ‘more or less’. The final نه is a clause‑level negation preparing the following clause (negation carried on to the next segment).
segments.s243-g134: This continues the negation: مرا is the experiencer/object pronoun (‘for me’ — here split in the word list), ایدر اکنون = ‘here now’ (spatial + temporal adverbials), کس و خویش = ‘anyone and kin’. The final نه negates existence: ‘for me here now there is neither anyone nor kin.’ Note the possessed/reflective suffix ش on خوی (w7) forming خویش ‘one’s own/kinsman’.
segments.s243-g135: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted, poetic order: چنین (‘thus/so’) modifies داد پاسخ (‘gave an answer’). پس (‘then’) and افراسیاب (the name) identify the speaker: ‘Then Afrasiab gave such an answer.’ داد is the past tense verb ‘gave’, and پاسخ is its object.
segments.s243-g136: که introduces reported speech (‘that’). بخت (‘fortune’) is the grammatical subject; بدت is a phrase meaning ‘bad for you’ where ت is the second‑person clitic attached to بد, and کرد is the past verb ‘made/caused’. بر تو شتاب is a prepositional phrase ‘haste/uprising upon you’ — idiomatically ‘haste/rapidity came upon you’ or ‘bad luck came upon you quickly’. The whole clause says that ill fortune swiftly befell you.
segments.s243-g9: بدانست is a past 3rd-person perfective ('he knew/realized'); the prefixed b- form is common in classical/Persian narrative. و simply links the clauses. ترسان is an adjective 'frightened/afraid' and شد is the past copula 'became' (adjective + شد = 'became frightened'). از + ٰجان خویش expresses the object/target of fear ('afraid of / for his life'); note that خویش is written here as two tokens (خوی + ش) where ش is the possessive suffix 'his'.
segments.s243-g10: شتابید is a simple past 'he hurried' (intransitive). نزدیک here functions adverbially/prepositionally ('toward/near, approaching') modifying حرکت; درمان خویش is 'his remedy' (درمان = remedy/cure) with خویش again split into خوی + ش (possessive). The clause says he moved quickly toward his own means of rescue.
segments.s243-g11: جز introduces an exception ('except'). آگاه کردن is a verbal construction: آگاه (aware) + کردن (light verb) = 'to inform / to make aware' (verbal noun/infinitive sense). ندید is past negative 'he saw not' and ایچ (classical) = 'anything/nothing' emphasizes the negation; رای ('counsel/plan') is the negated noun. Word order here (جز X ندید ایچ رای) ≈ 'he saw no plan except X.'
segments.s243-g12: دوان is a verb meaning 'he ran/ rushed' (simple past/action). از پس پرده is a fixed spatial phrase 'from behind the curtain' (از = from; پس = behind/after; پرده = curtain). برداشت پای is a light verbal phrase where برداشت (took/lifted) + پای (foot) = 'he lifted his foot / stepped forward' (i.e. stepped out).
segments.s243-g13: بیامد is past 'he came'. بر here functions as a directional preposition 'to/toward' before شاه ترکان ('the king of the Turks'). بگفت is past 'he said' (classical narrative past). The sequence is a straightforward narrative: he came to the king and spoke.
segments.s243-g14: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'. دختت is 'your daughter' (دخت + ـت possessive). ز (classical form of از) = 'from', so ز ایران = 'from Iran'. گزیدست is an archaic/perfect form (گزید + است contracted) meaning 'has chosen'; جفت is 'mate/spouse'. The clause reports what he told the king: that the king's daughter has chosen a spouse from Iran.
segments.s243-g15: جهانجوی is a compound noun 'world-seeker' (an ambitious person) used as the subject. کرد is the past 'made/did' and combines with یاد ('remembrance/mention') plus از جهاندار ('of the sovereign') to mean 'made mention of the sovereign' or 'recalled the sovereign.' Word order is Subject + کرد + از + object (یاد از جهاندار).
segments.s243-g16: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. گفتی here reports what 'you said' (classical past). که introduces the reported clause. بیدست is an adjective 'handless/helpless' (بےدست = without hand/defenceless). هنگام = 'at the time/at the moment'. باد at the end is the classical optative particle ('may / would') used to mark a wished-for or potential reading; the full sense is 'you said that he would be / might be helpless at the crucial moment.'
segments.s243-g105: از ایران بجنگ گراز آمدم is arranged as a short purpose/location clause: از ایران 'from Iran' (prepositional phrase), بجنگ + noun (بجنگ گراز) expresses purpose 'to fight a boar' using the older infinitival/oblique construction بجنگ, and آمدم is the past first‑person verb 'I came'. Word order is topical prepositional phrase → purpose phrase → verb.
segments.s243-g106: بدین جشن توران فراز آمدم: بدین is a fused preposition + demonstrative (به + این) meaning 'to/for this', جشن is the noun 'feast/celebration' governed by that prepositional phrase, توران is the place name 'Turan', فراز here functions adverbially 'up/on high' or 'upwards', and آمدم is again 'I came' (past first person). The phrase means movement 'I came up into Turan for this feast.'
segments.s243-g107: ز بهر یکی باز گم بوده را is a purposive/causal phrase: ز بهر = 'for the sake of' (ز used here with the sense 'because of/for'), یکی باز = 'a certain/one hawk' (یکی as 'a certain'), گم بوده is a perfect/periphrastic description 'had been lost' (بوده marking past state), and را is the object marker highlighting this noun phrase as the reason/patient in what follows.
segments.s243-g108: برانداختم مهربان دوده را: برانداختم is the past first‑person verb 'I drove off/removed/threw away', followed by the object مهربان دوده را 'the kind/gentle smoke' (مهربان qualifying دوده). را marks the definite object. The verb takes the preceding 'for the sake of …' phrase as context/reason.
segments.s243-g109: بزیر یکی سرو رفتم بخواب: بزیر is a locative adverb 'under/beneath', یکی سرو 'a certain cypress' (یکی = a certain), رفتم is the past 'I went', and بخواب is an infinitival/serial complement 'to sleep' (literal 'I went to sleep' construction). Word order places the location first, then the motion + purpose.
segments.s243-g110: که تا سایه دارد مرا ز آفتاب: که … تا is a subordinate clause giving cause/purpose—که can be 'that/because' and تا here 'so that/so as to' (together often signal 'because' or 'for that it'), سایه دارد = 'it has shade', مرا is the first‑person object 'me' (here split in the text), ز آفتاب = 'from the sun' (ز = from). The clause explains why he chose that spot (it afforded shade from the sun).
segments.s243-g111: پری دربیامد بگسترد پر: پری 'a fairy' is the subject, دربیامد is a classical/poetic compound meaning 'came in/appeared', and بگسترد پر is a verb + object/phrase 'spread (her) wing/feather' (بگسترد = 'she spread', پر = 'wing/feather'). The verbs are narrative past actions performed by the fairy.
segments.s243-g112: مرا اندر آورد خفته ببر: مرا (object clitic 'me', here orthographically split) + را marks the direct object 'me'; اندر آورد is 'brought in / carried into' (آورد 'bring' with اندر 'in, within'); خفته is the participial/adjectival 'asleep', and ببر here functions as a verb meaning 'carry/take' (contextually past‑narrative: 'and carried me off asleep'). The clause means the fairy brought him inside and carried him away while he was sleeping.
segments.s243-g33: زمانه (subject) is used in the sense of 'fate/fortune' and comes first; چرا بندد is a question ('why does ... bind?'), with بندد a classical 3rd-person singular present (or gnomic) verb 'binds'. این بند من is the object phrase 'this bond/fetter of mine' where بند (fetter) is followed by the possessor من (poetic order). The clause is a straightforward S-V-O order typical of older Persian.
segments.s243-g34: This is a noun phrase that specifies what binds him: غم (sorrow/grief) followed by two coordinated objects linked by و. شهر ایران is a genitive-like phrase 'the city of Iran' (no ezāfe is written because of poetic compression), and فرزند من 'my child' follows, with من as possessor.
segments.s243-g35: برو is an imperative 'go'. با سواران هشیار is a prepositional phrase 'with alert riders' (noun + adjective), placed after the verb. سر here is a short word meaning 'head/chief' used adverbially or emphatically before the following verb phrase (it heads into the command to take the lead or be vigilant) and links directly to نگه‌دار in the next segment.
segments.s243-g36: نگه‌دار functions as an imperative/command 'keep watch, guard' (in older Persian forms the noun and verb shapes can overlap). مر is the clitic/old form of مرا 'me', marking the beneficiary/goal ('for me'). کاخ را uses را as the definite/object marker, and بام و در specify the places to guard ('the roof and the gate'). Word order here is verb + object phrase with the object marker ra attached to کاخ.
segments.s243-g37: نگر is an imperative 'look/watch'. تا که introduces a temporal clause 'until', and بینی is the 2nd-person form 'you see' (subjunctive/present used in classical Persian after conjunctions). بکاخ is the contracted prepositional form be+kaakh 'at/to the palace', and اندرا is the proper name 'Andra' (object of the clause).
segments.s243-g38: ببند is an imperative 'bind/close'. The coordinating و 'and' links the next actions. کشانش is the verb stem کشان 'dragging' plus the enclitic ش 'him' (i.e., 'drag him'), used here as part of a verbal sequence. بیار is an imperative 'bring'. ایدرا is an archaic adverb/particle meaning roughly 'thereupon' or 'hither/then' that closes the command.
segments.s243-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گرسیوز is the subject name. آمد is the simple past 'came'. بنزدیک is a prefixed locative 'be-nazdik' meaning 'near', and در here is 'the gate/door' (the place he approached). The order is typical: temporal conjunction + subject + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s243-g40: از introduces source 'from the iwan/porch'. خروش آمد is a nominal clause 'a cry/uproar arose'. The added nouns نوش و خور ('drink and eating/feasting') are coordinated and appended to indicate the nature of the tumult; آمد governs the whole sequence, so the phrase means that a roar and the sounds (or acts) of eating and drinking came from the iwan.
segments.s243-g169: بفرمای is a polite imperative/command (an order: “order/let it be done”). داری here functions as the progressive auxiliary (classical/periphrastic use of داشتن + infinitive) marking ongoing/causal action: داری زدن = “have/keep (them) beat/strike.” زدن is the infinitive “to strike/beat.” پیش در is a locative phrase “before/in front of the door/gate” (پیش = before/in the presence of; در = gate/door).
segments.s243-g170: که introduces a purpose clause here (“so that/so as to”). باشد is a jussive/subjunctive form of بودن meaning “may be / let there be” (used to express the intended result). ز هر سو is a poetic prepositional phrase “from every side/direction” (ز = from; هر سو = every side). برو رهگذر is literal “passerby goes/comes” (برو used as ‘go/come’), i.e. “passersby (will) come” as the result of the beating. The clause therefore expresses purpose/result.
segments.s243-g171: نگون‌بخت را places the unfortunate person as a definite object (نگون‌بخت = “the ill‑fated/unfortunate one”; را = object marker). زنده modifies the object (“alive”). بر دار کن is an imperative construction: بر دار = “upon the gallows,” دار = “gallows,” and کن is the imperative “do!” — together “hang (him) alive.” Word order is object + verbal phrase with imperative.
segments.s243-g172: وزو is an archaic/contracted sequence (written here together) meaning ‘and from him / و از او’ — effectively “and from him.” نیز = “also/too.” با من is the prepositional phrase “to/with me” but here marks the indirect object of bringing speech. مگردان is a negative imperative (م‑ + گردان) meaning “do not turn/bring/return” (context: “do not bring/relay”). سخن = “speech/words.” Altogether: “and do not bring any words from him to me.”
segments.s243-g173: بدان is an imperative/address form built on دان (to know/attend to) with the prefix ب‑ giving the sense “see to it / take care / be sure.” تا introduces a purpose/result clause “so that / in order that.” ز ایرانیان = “from/among the Iranians.” زین سپس is a temporal phrase “from this time on / henceforth” (زین here is archaic ‘this/now’; سپس = then/afterwards). The whole segment issues the injunction’s temporal/scope frame.
segments.s243-g174: نیارد here stands in place of an archaic negative/resultive verb form meaning “let not come / let not go” (the supplied lemma senses do not match the usage; treat as a negative jussive). بتوران = “to/into Turan.” نگه کرد کس is a periphrastic expression meaning “let no one keep/watch” or colloquially “so that no person (does it)” — نگه = “watch/keeping,” کرد = past/causative “made/did,” کس = “person/anyone.” Together the clause expresses the intended prohibition: no one of the Iranians shall go into Turan thereafter.
segments.s243-g175: کشیدندش is the past plural verb کشیدند (“they dragged/pulled”) with the enclitic object pronoun ‑ش = “him.” از پیش افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase “from/before Afrasiab / in the presence of Afrasiab” (از = from; پیش = before/in front of; افراسیاب = proper name). The verb is past, third‑person plural, and the object pronoun is attached to the verb.
segments.s243-g176: This is a descriptive verbless clause in which دل از درد خسته = “(his) heart, weary from pain” (دل = heart; از درد = from pain; خسته = weary). دو دیده پر آب is a short nominal phrase “(his) two eyes full of water” (دیده = eye; پر = full (of); آب = water). The juxtaposition gives a mournful physical description; adjectives and phrases follow the nouns as modifiers in classical Persian.
segments.s243-g177: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' that precedes the verb آمد (past 'came'); بدر is a poetic epithet ('the full moon') placed before the name as an image, and خسته‌دل is a compound adjective after the name describing Bijan's state ('one with a weary heart'). Word order is typical for older Persian poetry: temporal marker + verb, then epithet + name + adjectival state.
segments.s243-g178: ز introduces a source/condition 'from' (poetic use); خون مژه is a compact image 'blood (on) the eyelashes' with مژه as the noun modified by خون; پای مانده is a participial phrase 'the foot/feet remained' (مانده = stayed/abided), and بگل (به‌گل) is a contracted prepositional phrase 'in/among the flowers' or 'with flowers' describing where the feet remained. This segment strings pictorial nouns and participle without an overt copula, as typical in poetry.
segments.s243-g179: همی before گفت gives a continuous or habitual sense ('kept saying' / 'still said'); اگر begins the conditional clause 'if'; بر سرم is the idiom 'upon my head' (= 'for me') with the enclitic -م marking 'my'; کردگار ('the Creator') is the agent/subject of the verb that follows (word order is poetic: the agent appears before the verb in the next segment). Note: گفت is used here as the verb 'said' in context (the annotation's listed senses don't include 'said').
segments.s243-g180: نوشتست is an archaic/perfect form 'has written' / 'it is written' (notice the older -ست ending); مردن is a verbal noun 'death' (object of نوشتست: 'has written death'); ببد is the copula 'is/was' and روزگار here means 'fate' or 'the times' as the predicative comment ('(then) fate is bad'). Word order places the verb نوشتست before its verbal-noun object (typical poetic arrangement).
segments.s243-g181: ز ... و ز sets up a coordinated list introduced by the preposition 'ز' ('from' / 'of'): 'from the gallows and from killing'. نترسم is the negative present/habitual of ترسیدن ('I do not fear'); همی again adds a continuous/emphatic sense ('I do not fear, I say / I keep saying'). Here کشتن is a verbal noun functioning as the thing feared ('death by killing').
segments.s243-g182: The clause opens with ز 'of/from' followed by گردان ایران ('the host/army of Iran' — گردان as 'troop' with the genitive Iran). بترسم is the positive counterpart to نترسم ('I do fear'), again with همی giving a continuing/emphatic tone. The change from ن- (negative) to ب- (verbal prefix) signals the shift from denial to admission of fear.
segments.s243-g183: که introduces an explanatory clause 'that / because'; the core clause is 'نامرد خواند مرا' (SOV: '(he) called me namard/coward') where خواند is the verb 'called/summoned' (here 'called'), and مرا (object 'me') is metrically split into م + را (seen in older/poetic texts). دشمنم ('my enemy') appears after the clause for emphasis/topic — here it is the agent: 'my enemy called me a coward.'
segments.s243-g184: ز ناخسته = 'against my will' or 'not willingly' (ز as 'by/from' with ناخسته indicating unwillingness). بردار کرده is a verbal sequence: بردار used here in a causative/take sense ('have taken / carried off') and کرده is the perfect participle 'has done/made', so بردار کرده تنم means literally 'they have made my body be carried' — idiomatically 'they have carried off my body.' تنم 'my body' is the affected noun (the annotation's senses for تنm do not list 'body', so it is treated as the patient).
segments.s243-g73: وگر introduces a conditional 'if'. خیزد is a third-person present/subjunctive verb 'rise/arise' following the particle; اندر جهان gives the location 'in the world'. رستخیز is a compound noun 'resurrection/uprising' placed after جهان (poetic word order) so the phrase reads 'if an uprising arises in the world'.
segments.s243-g74: نبیند is a negative/third-person-verb form 'will not see' (classical ordering allows verb before subject). کسی here is indefinite used with negation = 'no one'. پشتم is the possessive/oblique 'my back' and اندر گریز is an adverbial phrase 'in/while flight' describing how the seeing would occur (i.e. no one will see my back while I flee).
segments.s243-g75: تو دانی is the present 'you know' with subject before verb. نیاکان و شاه مرا lists two items known by the addressee: 'ancestors and (my) king'. Here the enclitic elements 'م' and 'را' attach to شاه (poetic spelling/segmentation) to mark possession/object; the clause therefore means 'you know my ancestors and my king.'
segments.s243-g76: میان یلان is 'among the heroes' (میان = among). پایگاه مرا is 'my base/standing' (پایگاه = position, stronghold) with the possessive/oblique element attached; the clause asserts the speaker's known place among the warriors. Note the classical word order places modifiers after the noun.
segments.s243-g77: وگر introduces a conditional. جنگ سازند is a plural verb form 'they make/raise war' (سازند = they make/compose). مر is the archaic object pronoun 'me' placed before the object phrase جنگ را for emphasis, and جنگ را is the definite object 'the war' (را marking the direct object). The whole clause: 'if they make war against me.'
segments.s243-g78: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. بشویم is a first-person-form used here as a volitional/future-like verb ('I will be / I shall'), functioning with the following verbal phrase. بخون is the stem 'sing/recite' (imperative/infinitival flavor in poetry) governing چنگ را 'the harp' as its object; together the clause means 'I will ever make the harp sing' or more idiomatically 'I will always make the harp sing (over blood)', i.e. celebrate in song (with blood).
segments.s243-g79: ز تورانیان is the prepositional phrase 'from the Turanians' (ز = from). من is the first-person subject 'I'. بدین خنجرا is the instrumental phrase 'with this dagger' — in the manuscript the preposition and demonstrative appear as ب + دین (poetic segmentation) followed by خنجرا 'dagger'. Together: 'I, from the Turanians, with this dagger...'.
segments.s243-g80: ببرم is the first-person verb 'I will take/cut off' (بردن used here with the sense of removing/severing). فراوان is a quantifier 'many/plentifully' modifying the following noun. سران را is the definite object 'the leaders/heads' marked by را. سرا functions as a noun/adverb 'to the house/hall' or as a terminal adverbial—here it completes the clause after the object (poetic, postpositional place), so the whole line reads 'I will sever many leaders' heads.'
